Factors Associated with Receipt of Next-Generation Sequencing Amongst NewYork-Presbyterian Cancer Patients

Weill Cornell Meyer Cancer Center Biostatistics Internship Analysis

Biostatistician: Mia Weller, Jalen Franklin

Principal Investigator: Rulla Tamimi, ScD

July 20, 2026

Study background information

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) is a form of genetic testing that can identify genetic mutations within a cancerous tumor (Ferreira-Gonzales et al., 2024). Knowledge of these mutations can better inform oncologists’ treatment decisions, facilitate earlier initiation of targeted therapies, and ultimately improve patient outcomes (Ferreira-Gonzales et al., 2024). However, not all cancer patients receive NGS. Existing literature has identified several demographic factors, including race and insurance status, as potential determinants of access to NGS (Ferreira-Gonzales et al., 2024). This project investigates factors associated with a patient’s likelihood of receiving NGS by analyzing data from Weill Cornell Medicine/NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital. While much of the existing literature has focused on demographic factors, this study evaluates these associations while controlling for relevant clinical characteristics to provide a more comprehensive assessment of disparities in access to NGS.

Using tumor registry data from the North American Association of Central Cancer Registries (NAACCR) collected between 2017 and 2020, we studied patients diagnosed with breast, lung, pancreatic, prostate, head and neck, and skin cancers. Descriptive statistics and bivariate analyses were used to compare patient characteristics across NGS testing priority groups (NGS_RANK). Variables of interest included race and ethnicity, sex, age, insurance status, marital status, Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), and receipt of NGS (NGS_TEST). Statistical tests included Pearson’s chi-square test, Fisher’s exact test, the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, and the Kruskal-Wallis rank-sum test, as appropriate.

Aims

  1. Characterize the demographic and clinical characteristics of patients with breast, lung, pancreatic, prostate, head and neck, and skin cancers overall and by indication (need severity) of next-generation sequencing (NGS).
  2. Compare characteristics of patients who received NGS and those who did not using bivariate statistical tests.
  3. Investigate associations of factors such as race and ethnicity, insurance, Social Vulnerability Index, and relevant clinical factors with receipt of tumor genomic sequencing using logistic regression models.
  4. Perform a sensitivity analysis using binomial generalized estimating equations (GEE) to explore factors related to sequencing while accounting for within-physician correlation arising from physician ordering tendencies.
  5. Map testing prevalence across NYC Census Tracts

Are patients disproportionately treated at different hospitals by cancer type?

Patients in this cohort were diagnosed/treated at one of three sites. The distribution below shows whether cancer type is confounded with hospital site - relevant both on its own (site differences in referral patterns, patient population, or NGS test availability) and because it drives how much oncologist-clustering data is available per cancer type in Section 4.

1. Descriptive statistics of patient characteristics by cancer type and testing severity indication

The table summarizes demographic, clinical, and socioeconomic characteristics by next-generation sequencing level. The next-generation sequencing levels were derived from a Cancer Genomics policy by Dr. Neal Lindeman, MD, on behalf of the Weill Cornell Medicine and New York Presbyterian faculty. Level 0 indicates no recommendation for NGS testing, Level 1 indicates the highest priority for testing, typically ordered by a pathologist, Level 2 indicates testing recommended by an oncologist, and Level 3 indicates testing considered on a case-by-case basis.

2. Comparing patient characteristics of those tested vs. not tested amongst those with some testing indication

Bivariate hypothesis testing is a way to examine relationships between variables, which we used to compare patient characteristics and those who received testing versus those who did not.

Breast cancer

Among 212 breast cancer patients with an indication for NGS testing only 9% received testing. There were no significant differences in tested and untested patients in regards to race, sex and marital status.

Lung cancer

Among 2,136 lung cancer patients with an indication for NGS testing 26% received testing. Tested patients were more likely to be Non-Hispanic White (52% and 36%) than Non-Hispanic Black (8.7% and 17%) or Non-Hispanic Asian (18% and 26%).

Pancreatic cancer

Among 944 pancreatic cancer patients with an indication for NGS testing 18% received testing. Tested patients were more likely to be Non-Hispanic White (56% vs. 34%) and less likely to be Non-Hispanic Asian (7.6% and 18%). Tested patients were also more likely to be married (58% and 48%) and lived in less socioeconomically burdened neighborhoods.

Among 242 prostate cancer patients with an indication for NGS testing 17% received testing. Tested patients were more likely to be Non-Hispanic White (71% and 41%) and less likely to be Non-Hispanic Black (9.5% and 23%).

3. Logistic Regression

Logistic regression was used to study the associations between demographic, clinical, and socioeconomic factors and the likelihood of receiving NGS testing. Race/ethnicity, primary payer, neighborhood social vulnerability, and NGS indication level were evaluated as effects on NGS testing for each cancer type.

All models below that include Race_Ethnicity are fit on the “Unknown” level dropped to NA, so patients with unknown race are excluded from race-adjusted models rather than treated as their own race category (an OR for “Unknown vs. White” has no clean causal interpretation). The table below reports how many eligible patients per cancer type are excluded on this basis, so that loss is visible rather than silent.

Primary site Number eligible Number unknown race Percent unknown race
Breast 212 5 2.4
Head & Neck 27 1 3.7
Lung 2136 108 5.1
Pancreas 944 56 5.9
Prostate 242 11 4.5
Skin 82 10 12.2

Lung

Lung Race + Insurance

For lung cancer, race/ethnicity and primary payer were significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing. Non-Hispanic Asian (OR 0.42, 95% CI 0.31–0.55), Non-Hispanic Black (OR 0.34, 95% CI 0.24–0.47), and Hispanic patients (OR 0.66, 95% CI 0.45–0.95) were less likely to receive testing compared to Non-Hispanic White patients, controlling for insurance, age at diagnosis, sex, and testing severity indication. Patients with Medicaid were also less likely to receive testing compared with those with private insurance (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.46–0.98), controlling for race, age at diagnosis, sex, and testing severity indication. In regards to socioeconomic status, patients from highly vulnerable areas were 42% less likely to receive NGS testing compared to those from low vulnerability areas (p=0.004).

Characteristic OR 95% CI p-value
Race/Ethnicity

    Non-Hispanic Black 0.34 0.24, 0.48 <0.001
    Hispanic 0.66 0.45, 0.95 0.027
Primary payer

Breast

Breast Race + Insurance

For breast cancer, race/ethnicity and primary payer were significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing. Non-Hispanic Black patients (OR 0.18, 95% CI 0.03–0.77) were 82% less likely to receive testing as compared to Non-Hispanic White patients, controlling for insurance, age at diagnosis, and testing severity. Patients with Medicare were 7 times more likely to receive testing compared with those with private insurance (OR 7.15, 95% CI 1.08-58.3), controlling for race, age at diagnosis, and testing severity.

Pancreatic

Pancreas Race + Insurance

For pancreatic cancer, race/ethnicity and primary payer were significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing. Non-Hispanic Asian (OR 0.27, 95% CI 0.14–0.49), Non-Hispanic Black (OR 0.45, 95% CI 0.27–0.72), and Hispanic patients (OR 0.37, 95% CI 0.20–0.64) were 41% less likely to receive testing compared to Non-Hispanic White patients, controlling for insurance, age at diagnosis, and sex. Patients with Medicaid were 72% less likely to receive testing as compared with those with private insurance (OR 0.28, 95% CI 0.13–0.58). In regards to socioeconomic status, patients from highly vulnerable areas and moderately vulnerable areas were 85% less likely to receive NGS testing compared to those from low vulnerability areas (p<0.001).

Prostate

Prostate Race + Insurance

For prostate cancer, race/ethnicity was the only factor significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing. Non-Hispanic Black (OR 0.19, 95% CI 0.05-0.57) patients were 81% less likely to receive testing as compared to Non-Hispanic White patients.

Head and Neck

Head & Neck has only 27 patients eligible for NGS testing, of whom just 2 received it - and both of those 2 happen to share the same race category (Non-Hispanic Black) and the same insurance category (Private Insurance). That means Race_Ethnicity or Primary.Payer alone already perfectly separates the outcome: no multivariable logistic regression can produce a stable, interpretable fit on this subgroup regardless of which covariates are included or dropped (we tried removing Sex first; the separation persists because it’s driven by Race_Ethnicity/Primary.Payer, not by having too many parameters). Rather than report coefficients with standard errors in the hundreds of thousands that convey false precision, we omit a regression model for Head & Neck entirely and refer to the descriptive/bivariate comparison in Section 2 above, which is the appropriate level of analysis this sample size can actually support.

Skin

Skin Race + Insurance

For skin cancer, primary payer was the only factor significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing. Patients with a type of insurance other than Medicaid, Medicare, or private insurance were 13 times as likely to receive testing as compared with those with private insurance (OR 13.0, 95% CI 1.65–196).

4. Accounting for clustering by treating oncologist

The models above (Section 3) treat every patient as an independent observation. That may be unlikely to hold: patients seen by the same oncologist plausibly share testing propensity through referral patterns, panel/formulary access, and individual ordering habits. If race/SES effects are partly proxying for “which oncologist a patient happened to see,” ignoring that clustering can understate standard errors and overstate how confident we should be in the demographic associations found above.

The following analysis constructs GEE models clustering on physician. Results are presented for lung, breast, and pancreatic cancers. Skin, head and neck, and prostate cancers were excluded because physician-level clustering models were unstable due to limited sample sizes and sparse physician-level variation, with many physicians contributing only a single patient.

A note on hospital site and oncologist coverage

Patients were diagnosed/treated at one of three sites (Hospital, derived from Reporting Facility): Weill Cornell Medical College (Manhattan), NY Hospital Queens, and NYP Brooklyn Methodist. The Epic-derived oncologist (PRIMARY_ONCOLOGIST_PROV_ID, built in the SQL from Epic visit data) is well populated at Manhattan (~83% resolved) but largely missing at Queens (~17% resolved) and Brooklyn (~48% resolved) - those two sites were not (fully) on Epic for the study period, so the gap is a site go-live artifact, not missing-at-random. Using Epic data alone for clustering would effectively make Section 4 a Manhattan-only analysis.

To avoid that, ONCOLOGIST_NPI combines two sources: the NAACCR registry’s own Physician--Follow-Up field (item description: “physician currently responsible for the patient’s medical care”), which is collected independent of EHR and stays ~99%/91-93% complete (name/NPI) at every site including Queens and Brooklyn; and, only where that’s still missing, the Epic-derived ID (prefixed EPIC_ to keep the two ID spaces distinct) as a fallback. This is the clustering variable used below. PRIMARY_ONCOLOGIST_PROV_ID remains in the dataset separately if a Manhattan-only sensitivity check against the more visit-count-precise Epic measure is wanted later.

Hospital Number eligible Percent EPIC oncologist Percent combined oncologist NPI
NY Hospital Queens 1053 11.1 93.1
NYP Brooklyn Methodist 784 32.4 95.3
Weill Cornell (Manhattan) 1806 84.6 99.7

We refit the “Race + Insurance” model for each cancer clustering on ONCOLOGIST_NPI using GEE models (Generalized Estimating Equations) to account for within-oncologist correlation.

Patients with no resolved ONCOLOGIST_NPI are dropped from these models - counts are reported per cancer type so the extent of that loss is visible rather than silent. Head & neck and skin are omitted here: even before oncologist-level clustering, those subgroups are too sparse (see Section 3 notes) to support an additional clustering term reliably.

Lung

After accounting for treating oncologist and controlling for the same covariates, Non-Hispanic Asian patients had a 33% reduced odds of testing as compared to Non-Hispanic White patients (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.50-0.91) and Non-Hispanic Black patients had a 48% reduced odds of testing as compared to Non-Hispanic White patients (OR 0.52, 95% CI 0.37-0.73).

1961 lung cancer patients (of those eligible for NGS) had a resolved treating oncologist, clustered across 761 distinct oncologists. Of those oncologists, 372 have only a single patient in this subset.

Breast

After accounting for treating oncologist, age is significantly associated with receipt of NGS testing, with each one-unit increase in age being associated with an 8% reduction in odds of NGS testing.

201 breast cancer patients (of those eligible for NGS) had a resolved treating oncologist, clustered across 109 distinct oncologists.

Pancreatic

After accounting for treating oncologist and controlling for covariates, Non-Hispanic Asian patients (OR 0.44, 95% CI 0.22-0.86) had a 56% decreased likelihood of receiving testing as compared to Non-Hispanic White patients. Patients with Medicaid (OR 0.47, 95% CI 0.24-0.89) had a 53% decreased likelihood of receiving testing as compared to patients with private insurance.

854 pancreatic cancer patients (of those eligible for NGS) had a resolved treating oncologist, clustered across 389 distinct oncologists. Note: NGS_RANK is a constant (all patients rank 1) within this pancreatic-eligible subset, so it’s dropped from the models below.

All Cancer Map

Testing rate by borough

Borough Total patients NGS tested patients NGS testing prevalence
Bronx 98 42 0.4286
Brooklyn 898 151 0.1682
Manhattan 552 267 0.4837
Queens 281 71 0.2527
Staten Island 71 33 0.4648

Conclusions

In conclusion, race/ethnicity, primary payer type, and social vulnerability index were consistently associated with a cancer patient’s likelihood of receiving NGS testing. Possible explanations behind this disparity could include a deeply-rooted mistrust of the medical system by black patients and limitations within the Medicare and medicaid system in coverage and access. One limitation of this study is its restriction to the Weill Cornell Medicine/New York-Presbyterian Hospital. It would be interesting to see if these findings could be replicated in a nationwide study. Findings could inform future studies on raising awareness of and medical literacy about NGS testing among both patients and oncologists.

Division of Biostatistics

Department of Population Health Sciences

Mia Weller, Jalen Franklin
